title: AP U.S. History SAQ Practice Exam 03

questions.prompt:
Source A: Original secondary interpretation written for this practice exam, not an actual quotation.
A historian of the American Revolution argues that Patriot resistance grew mainly from constitutional conflict within the British Empire. According to this interpretation, colonial leaders challenged Parliament’s authority because they believed taxation, representation, and imperial administration threatened the rights of English subjects.
Source B: Original secondary interpretation written for this practice exam, not an actual quotation.
A historian of revolutionary society argues that the Revolution cannot be understood only as an elite constitutional dispute. According to this interpretation, ordinary artisans, farmers, women, enslaved people, and local committees helped turn imperial protest into a broader political movement that changed expectations about participation and rights.
(a) Describe one major difference between the two interpretations of the American Revolution.
(b) Explain one piece of historical evidence that supports the argument in Source A.
(c) Explain one piece of historical evidence that supports the argument in Source B.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Describes a valid difference, such as Source A emphasizing constitutional and imperial disputes among colonial leaders, while Source B emphasizes popular mobilization and broader social participation.
(b) 1 point: Explains relevant evidence supporting Source A, such as colonial opposition to the Stamp Act, “virtual representation,” the Declaratory Act, taxation without representation, colonial assemblies’ claims to authority, or debates over Parliament’s power.
(c) 1 point: Explains relevant evidence supporting Source B, such as nonimportation movements, Sons of Liberty activity, women’s boycotts and homespun production, committees of correspondence, crowd actions, petitions by enslaved people, or local militia mobilization.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should avoid reducing the Revolution to only taxes or only democratic ideals. It should show how constitutional arguments by colonial elites interacted with broader forms of popular political action and social mobilization.

questions.prompt:
Source: Original simulated source based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
A Mexican American high school student writing to a local school board after student walkouts, Los Angeles, 1968.
“Our parents came here to work and build a future, but in school we are told that our language is a problem and our history is invisible. We do not ask for special treatment. We ask for teachers who expect us to graduate, counselors who do not send us away from college, and classes that show Mexican Americans as part of this country’s story. If the district calls this disorder, it has not listened to the order we have lived under.”
(a) Identify one historical development that shaped the situation described in the source.
(b) Explain how the author’s point of view likely influenced the argument made in the source.
(c) Explain one way the source reflects broader civil rights activism in the United States during the 1960s and 1970s.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a relevant development, such as the Chicano Movement, East Los Angeles school walkouts, educational inequality, Mexican American civil rights activism, bilingual education debates, or broader rights movements of the 1960s.
(b) 1 point: Explains that the author’s perspective as a Mexican American student influenced the argument by emphasizing school discrimination, language, curriculum, low expectations, college access, and recognition of Mexican American identity.
(c) 1 point: Explains a broader connection, such as youth activism, ethnic pride movements, challenges to institutional discrimination, demands for educational reform, Latino/a civil rights organizing, Black Power influence, women’s rights, Native activism, or Asian American activism.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should connect the source to civil rights activism beyond the Black freedom struggle without ignoring common themes such as equal access, institutional discrimination, and community organizing. Strong responses will explain why education became a key site of protest.

questions.prompt:
Source: Described data table created for this practice exam.
The table is titled “Selected Trends in Southern Agriculture, 1870–1900.”
Indicator | 1870 | 1885 | 1900
Share of Southern farms operated by tenants or sharecroppers | about 25% | about 45% | more than 50%
Cotton acreage | expanding | expanding rapidly | very high
Average crop diversity on cotton farms | limited | declining | very limited
Farmers reporting debt to merchants or landlords | common | very common | widespread
Membership in farmers’ alliances or agrarian protest groups | low | growing | significant in many areas
The table notes that the figures are approximate and are intended to show broad regional patterns after Reconstruction.
(a) Describe one economic trend in the post-Reconstruction South reflected in the table.
(b) Explain one cause of the economic trend described in part (a).
(c) Explain one political or social response to the agricultural conditions reflected in the table.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Describes a valid trend, such as the growth of tenancy and sharecropping, dependence on cotton, declining crop diversity, widespread debt, or increasing agrarian protest.
(b) 1 point: Explains a relevant cause, such as lack of land ownership among freedpeople and poor whites, crop-lien credit systems, falling cotton prices, merchant control of supplies, limited access to capital, or the collapse of Reconstruction-era land redistribution hopes.
(c) 1 point: Explains a relevant response, such as the Farmers’ Alliance, Populist Party organizing, calls for railroad regulation, demands for currency reform, cooperative buying and selling, biracial organizing attempts, or political backlash against agrarian protest.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should connect agricultural data to systems of credit, labor, and landownership. It should avoid treating sharecropping as simply a personal choice and should explain why economic hardship contributed to organized agrarian protest.
